questions.q: In which scientific field did Birbal Sahni do his pioneering work?
questions.explanation: Birbal Sahni was a palaeobotanist, a scientist who studies the fossils of ancient plants.

questions.q: What does a palaeobotanist like Birbal Sahni primarily study?
questions.explanation: Palaeobotany is the study of fossil plants, which is the area in which Sahni made his name.

questions.q: The research institute that bears Birbal Sahni's name today specialises in what?
questions.explanation: He founded the institute of palaeosciences in Lucknow that now carries his name, the Birbal Sahni Institute of Palaeosciences.

questions.q: Birbal Sahni was a scientist from which country?
questions.explanation: Birbal Sahni was an Indian palaeobotanist who studied India's fossil plants.

questions.q: In which Indian city is the Birbal Sahni Institute of Palaeosciences located?
questions.explanation: The institute he founded is located in Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh.

questions.q: Fossil plants are most often preserved in which kind of material?
questions.explanation: Plant fossils are typically preserved in rock and coal deposits, which palaeobotanists like Sahni examined.

questions.q: Birbal Sahni's nickname on his card describes him as a 'Reader of Ancient ___'.
questions.explanation: He is called the 'Reader of Ancient Plants' because he interpreted the fossil flora of the geological past.

questions.q: Which natural specimens were central to Birbal Sahni's research?
questions.explanation: Sahni's career centred on studying fossilised plant remains preserved in India's rocks.

questions.q: Birbal Sahni is best remembered as a founder of an institution dedicated to what broad area?
options: The study of ancient life and Earth
questions.explanation: He founded an institute devoted to palaeosciences, the study of ancient life and Earth history.

questions.q: Studying fossils helps scientists like Birbal Sahni understand what?
questions.explanation: Fossils reveal the life of the geological past, which is exactly what palaeobotany seeks to reconstruct.

questions.q: In which year was Birbal Sahni born?
questions.explanation: Birbal Sahni was born in 1891 in Bhera, in present-day Pakistan.

questions.q: Birbal Sahni pursued advanced studies in botany at which famous British university?
questions.explanation: He studied at the University of Cambridge, where he worked under the botanist A. C. Seward.

questions.q: At which Indian university did Birbal Sahni serve for most of his career as professor and head of botany?
questions.explanation: Sahni spent most of his career at the University of Lucknow, where he was professor and head of the botany department.

questions.q: Which influential palaeobotanist at Cambridge was Birbal Sahni's mentor?
questions.explanation: Sahni studied under Albert Charles Seward, a leading palaeobotanist at Cambridge.

questions.q: In what year was Birbal Sahni elected a Fellow of the Royal Society (FRS) of London?
questions.explanation: He was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1936, a rare honour for an Indian scientist of his time.

questions.q: Birbal Sahni founded the Birbal Sahni Institute (originally the Institute of Palaeobotany) in which year?
questions.explanation: He established the institute in 1946; it was later named in his honour after his death.

questions.q: Besides plants, Birbal Sahni also took a keen interest in which other field that used scientific dating?
questions.explanation: Sahni studied ancient coins and coin-moulds, applying scientific methods to numismatics.

questions.q: Birbal Sahni's father, Ruchi Ram Sahni, was himself notable as a teacher of which subject?
questions.explanation: His father Ruchi Ram Sahni was a noted science educator and chemist who inspired Birbal's scientific interests.

questions.q: Birbal Sahni was a founding figure and first president of which Indian scientific body, the Palaeobotanical Society?
options: A society devoted to fossil plant research
questions.explanation: He helped found and led the Palaeobotanical Society, the body devoted to fossil plant research in India.

questions.q: Much of Sahni's work focused on the fossil flora of which ancient supercontinent that included peninsular India?
questions.explanation: He extensively studied the Gondwana flora, the ancient plant life of the southern supercontinent that included India.

questions.q: In which year did Birbal Sahni die, shortly after laying the foundation stone of his new institute?
questions.explanation: Sahni died in 1949 (April), only days after the foundation stone of the new institute building was laid.

questions.q: Which genus of fossil plant did Birbal Sahni describe and is named after him?
questions.explanation: The fossil wood genus Sahnioxylon was named in his honour for his palaeobotanical contributions.

questions.q: In which town, located in present-day Pakistan, was Birbal Sahni born?
questions.explanation: He was born in 1891 in Bhera, in the Shahpur district of present-day Punjab, Pakistan.

questions.q: Birbal Sahni proposed and studied a new group of fossil plants combining features of two major groups; what did he name this order?
questions.explanation: Sahni established the Pentoxyleae (Pentoxylales), a distinctive group of Mesozoic gymnosperms, based on fossils from the Rajmahal Hills.

questions.q: Fossils that formed the basis of Sahni's Pentoxyleae work came chiefly from which hills in eastern India?
questions.explanation: The Jurassic plant fossils of the Rajmahal Hills in Bihar/Jharkhand were central to his Pentoxyleae studies.

questions.q: Birbal Sahni earned his doctorate (DSc) from which institution?
questions.explanation: He was awarded the Doctor of Science (DSc) degree by the University of London in 1919.

questions.q: Sahni's research on ancient coin-moulds at Rohtak helped clarify the minting techniques of which period of Indian history?
options: The early historic period (around the turn of the Common Era)
questions.explanation: His study of die-struck coins and casting moulds shed light on early historic Indian minting around the turn of the Common Era.

questions.q: Before settling at Lucknow, Birbal Sahni briefly held a professorship of botany at which other Indian university?
questions.explanation: He served as the first professor of botany at Banaras Hindu University before moving to the University of Lucknow.

questions.q: Who laid the foundation stone of Birbal Sahni's institute building in Lucknow in 1949, an event preceding his death?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru laid the foundation stone of the institute's new building in April 1949, days before Sahni died.

questions.q: Birbal Sahni's wife, Savitri Sahni, was important to his legacy chiefly because she did what after his death?
options: Continued to guide and support the institute he founded
Won a Nobel Prize in Chemistry
questions.explanation: After his death, Savitri Sahni helped sustain and develop the institute he founded, ensuring his legacy continued.
